task: I’ve got a job lined up where one crew is ready to start before all the sign-offs are in, and there’s pressure to keep the schedule moving. What’s the best way to handle that moment so the work stays organized and the site doesn’t get thrown off?

turns.agent_response:
Pause it and make the decision gate explicit.

A good way to handle the moment is:

- Acknowledge the pressure: “I know you’re ready to keep moving.”
- State the missing sign-off clearly: “The [specific check] hasn’t been signed off yet.”
- Put the decision back with the responsible party: “I need a clear **go** or **stop** from [inspector/office/owner] before the crew moves.”
- Hold the crew at the boundary until you get that answer.

That keeps it firm, avoids confusion, and protects you from rework or a bad sequence.

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a one-line text you can send, or  
2. a quick site-crew script for saying it in person.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s the right shape. I’d want it tighter though — who gets told first, and what do I do with the crew in the meantime so they’re not just standing around burning time? Give me a clean in-person script and a fallback if the owner starts pushing to keep going anyway.

turns.agent_response:
Tell **the responsible sign-off person first**: inspector, GC, or whoever owns that approval.  
Then tell the **crew lead** to hold at the boundary and switch them to **prep work that doesn’t cross the line**.

**In-person script:**
“Stop here. We’re missing the sign-off on [item]. I’ve already called it in. Keep the crew on prep, cleanup, layout, or staging until we get a go.”

**If the owner pushes to keep going:**
“I can’t let this move past the hold point without the sign-off. If you want it to proceed, I need that approval first.”

**While they wait:**
- stage material
- clean the work area
- verify layout
- prep tools/equipment
- do work that stays clear of the hold point

If you want, I can turn that into a **30-second foreman script** and a **one-line text** you can send right away.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Better, but I’d tighten it more and make it harder to argue with.

First call: whoever can actually clear the hold. Inspector, GC, office — whoever owns the sign-off. Not the crew, not the owner, unless they’re the one with the authority.

Then the foreman. Keep the crew busy on stuff that won’t contaminate the hold point.

In person:

“Hold up. We’re not past sign-off yet. I’ve got the approval side working. Until that clears, keep them on prep, cleanup, staging, layout, whatever stays outside the line.”

If the owner starts pushing:

“I’m not gambling the job on a bad sequence. Once the sign-off lands, we move. Before that, we stay clean.”

That’s the clean way. No debate, no long speech. The crew hears the boundary, the owner hears you’re not folding.

If you want, I can make it even shorter — like a 10-second version for the site and a separate line for the owner.

turns.agent_response:
First call: **whoever can clear the hold**. Inspector, GC, office — the authority on the sign-off.

Then the foreman: keep the crew on work that stays **outside the hold point**.

**In person:**
“Hold up. We’re not past sign-off yet. I’m getting the approval cleared. Until then, keep them on prep, cleanup, staging, layout — nothing that touches the hold point.”

**If the owner pushes:**
“I’m not risking the sequence. Once the sign-off lands, we go. Before that, we stay clean.”

**If you need the crew busy:**
- prep
- cleanup
- staging
- layout
- tool/equipment setup

No debate. Clear boundary, keep moving on safe work, wait for the approval.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s it. Clean, hard to argue with.

That line on the owner is the one — "I’m not risking the sequence." Simple. No sermon.

The crew can stay busy enough on prep, cleanup, staging, layout. Keeps them out of trouble and off the clock waste.

Good.
